bytes.topic: Warehouse vs Database — OLAP vs OLTP, columnar vs row storage, analytical workloads
bytes.title: BigQuery — Why It's a Warehouse, Not a Database (OLAP vs OLTP Under the Hood)
bytes.body:
BigQuery is built on Google's Dremel engine using Capacitor columnar storage — not a row-oriented OLTP database. Each column is stored and compressed independently across Colossus (Google's distributed filesystem). When you run `SELECT price, quantity FROM orders`, BigQuery reads only those two columns. In PostgreSQL, the same query reads entire row pages. This is the core OLAP advantage.

Because on-demand pricing bills by bytes scanned, columnar pruning is also a cost lever. `SELECT *` on a 1 TB table scans 1 TB. Selecting 2 of 50 columns might scan 40 GB.

Clustering extends this further. `CLUSTER BY category` co-locates rows sharing the same value into contiguous storage blocks. A `WHERE category = 'Electronics'` filter then reads block-level min/max metadata and skips irrelevant blocks entirely — this is called block pruning. Partition pruning works at a coarser level via `_PARTITIONTIME` or a `DATE` column.

BigQuery rewards wide, denormalized schemas with partitioning and clustering. Normalized OLTP schemas with many joins and small lookups fight the grain of columnar execution — JOIN spills to shuffle, slot utilization spikes, and costs balloon. Design for analytical access patterns from day one.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Create a partition + cluster table optimized for OLAP scans
CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE `project.dataset.orders`
PARTITION BY DATE(created_at)
CLUSTER BY customer_id, status
AS SELECT * FROM `project.dataset.orders_raw`;

-- BigQuery performs partition pruning + block pruning automatically
SELECT
  customer_id,
  SUM(total_amount) AS revenue
FROM `project.dataset.orders`
WHERE DATE(created_at) BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-03-31'
  AND status = 'completed'
GROUP BY customer_id;

-- Estimate bytes scanned before committing (dry run):
-- bq query --dry_run --use_legacy_sql=false 'SELECT ...'

bytes.topic: Snowflake — virtual warehouses, zero-copy cloning, time travel, data sharing
bytes.title: BigQuery vs Snowflake — Virtual Warehouses, Zero-Copy Cloning, Time Travel & Data Sharing Decoded
bytes.body:
BigQuery developers evaluating Snowflake encounter concepts that look familiar but behave very differently.

Snowflake's **virtual warehouses** are manually sized compute clusters (XS–6XL) billed per second of runtime. Unlike BigQuery's serverless model—where slots scale automatically and you're billed per byte scanned or via slot reservations—you must explicitly START/SUSPEND warehouses. An idle warehouse costs nothing; a forgotten running one bleeds budget.

**Zero-copy cloning** (`CREATE TABLE orders_clone CLONE orders`) is Snowflake-native: it duplicates metadata only, sharing micro-partitions until writes diverge. BigQuery has no equivalent—`bq cp` physically copies data, so clones carry full storage cost. BigQuery table snapshots (`CREATE SNAPSHOT TABLE`) approximate this but are read-only.

**Time travel** exists in both. BigQuery uses `FOR SYSTEM_TIME AS OF` with a 7-day default window. Snowflake's `AT (OFFSET => -N)` syntax extends to 90 days on Enterprise tier—a meaningful operational difference for incident recovery.

**Data sharing** in Snowflake is direct account-to-account with no data movement. BigQuery's Analytics Hub is the closest analog, routing listings through Google's managed marketplace rather than peer-to-peer.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- BigQuery: query orders as they existed 24 hours ago
SELECT
  order_id,
  status,
  total_amount
FROM `myproject.sales.orders`
FOR SYSTEM_TIME AS OF TIMESTAMP_SUB(CURRENT_TIMESTAMP(), INTERVAL 24 HOUR)
WHERE status = 'pending'
ORDER BY total_amount DESC
LIMIT 100;

bytes.topic: BigQuery — serverless model, slot reservations, partitioning, clustering, cost control
bytes.title: BigQuery Cost Control — Slots, Partitions, and Clustering Done Right
bytes.body:
BigQuery's serverless model bills on bytes scanned, not compute time — so query design directly drives your invoice. On-demand pricing charges ~$6.25/TB scanned; flat-rate slot reservations (via BigQuery Reservations API) make sense above ~100TB/month by giving predictable capacity through FLEX, STANDARD, or ENTERPRISE commitments assigned to projects via reservation assignments.

Partitioning and clustering are your first line of defense. Partition on a DATE/TIMESTAMP column (or an integer range) so the query engine can prune entire storage blocks before scanning. Clustering on up to 4 columns within each partition lets BigQuery skip blocks based on column min/max metadata — most effective on high-cardinality filter columns like user_id or event_type.

Always filter on the partition column before joining or aggregating; a missing partition filter forces a full table scan regardless of clustering. Use INFORMATION_SCHEMA.PARTITIONS to audit partition pruning efficiency, and the Jobs API (jobs.get → statistics.query.totalBytesProcessed vs. totalBytesBilled) to verify savings. Enable require_partition_filter at the table level to enforce pruning across all queries.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Enforce partition pruning + clustering savings
CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE analytics.events
PARTITION BY DATE(occurred_at)
CLUSTER BY user_id, event_type
OPTIONS (require_partition_filter = TRUE)
AS SELECT * FROM staging.raw_events WHERE FALSE;

-- Verify bytes scanned vs billed for a query job
SELECT
  job_id,
  total_bytes_processed / pow(1024,4) AS tb_processed,
  total_bytes_billed   / pow(1024,4) AS tb_billed
FROM `region-us`.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS_BY_PROJECT
WHERE creation_time > TIMESTAMP_SUB(CURRENT_TIMESTAMP(), INTERVAL 1 DAY)
ORDER BY tb_billed DESC
LIMIT 20;

bytes.topic: Redshift — distribution keys, sort keys, VACUUM, ANALYZE, RA3 nodes
bytes.title: BigQuery vs Redshift — Distribution Keys, Sort Keys, VACUUM, and What BigQuery Abstracts Away
bytes.body:
BigQuery handles what Redshift forces you to manage manually. In Redshift, you must define a DISTKEY (hash, even, or all) to control how rows are spread across nodes — poor choices cause data skew and slow joins. SORTKEY (compound or interleaved) determines on-disk ordering for range scans. After bulk loads or deletes, you must run VACUUM to reclaim space and re-sort rows, then ANALYZE to refresh statistics. RA3 nodes decouple compute from storage using Redshift Managed Storage (RMS), so you scale them independently — but you still own the tuning.

BigQuery does none of this. Colossus handles distributed storage automatically; Dremel parallelizes execution across slots without node-level placement decisions. The closest BigQuery primitive is CLUSTER BY, which sorts data within partitions for pruning — no manual VACUUM, no ANALYZE, no distribution strategy. Capacitor columnar encoding and automatic stats collection happen server-side. If you're evaluating Redshift or reading someone else's DDL, knowing this mapping prevents you from searching for BigQuery equivalents that simply don't exist.

bytes.topic: Databricks — Delta Lake, Unity Catalog, notebooks, Spark integration, Photon engine
bytes.title: BigQuery to Databricks — Delta Lake, Unity Catalog, and Photon Through a BigQuery Lens
bytes.body: BigQuery users moving to Databricks face a lakehouse paradigm shift: storage (Delta Lake on object storage) and compute (Spark clusters) are decoupled — there's no serverless slot abstraction managing this invisibly for you. Delta Lake replaces BigQuery's native Capacitor format. It brings ACID transactions and time travel via `VERSION AS OF` / `TIMESTAMP AS OF`, directly analogous to BigQuery's `FOR SYSTEM_TIME AS OF`. Data skipping uses Z-ordering (`OPTIMIZE ... ZORDER BY`) — BigQuery handles equivalent pruning automatically via clustering columns. Unity Catalog maps to BigQuery's project → dataset → table hierarchy as catalog → schema → table, but adds column-level masking policies and row filters that BigQuery implements via column-level security and authorized views. The Photon engine is Databricks' Dremel counterpart — a vectorized C++ runtime accelerating SQL and DataFrame workloads with no code changes required. The critical operational gap: BigQuery's autoscaling and automatic table maintenance are your problem in Databricks. You must size clusters, run `OPTIMIZE` for file compaction, and schedule `VACUUM` to expire stale snapshots — none of this is managed for you.

bytes.topic: Data Modeling — star schema, snowflake schema, wide tables, dimensional modeling
bytes.title: BigQuery Data Modeling — Why Wide Tables Beat Star Schemas
bytes.body:
BigQuery's columnar storage changes the calculus on dimensional modeling. Star and snowflake schemas made sense for row-based OLTP engines where normalization reduced storage. In BigQuery, JOINs cause shuffle across distributed slots, and on-demand billing charges per bytes scanned — not per row or join. Wide, denormalized tables eliminate that overhead entirely.

Instead of a classic snowflake (orders → customers → segments → regions), flatten dimensions into the fact table using BigQuery's native STRUCT and ARRAY types. Nested fields are co-located on disk, so customer.region costs nothing extra alongside order_id — and column pruning means you only scan what you SELECT.

Combine PARTITION BY DATE(order_date) with CLUSTER BY on your most-filtered dimensions (region, product_category). This gives you dimension-key lookups without maintaining a separate dimension table or surrogate key index.

Reserve separate dimension tables only when multiple fact tables share the same dimension and you need a single update path — then use BigQuery materialized views to pre-join them back for query performance. Avoid snowflake schemas entirely; each normalization level adds a shuffle join that burns slots and inflates latency.
bytes.code_snippet:
CREATE TABLE `project.dataset.orders_wide`
PARTITION BY DATE(order_date)
CLUSTER BY region, product_category
AS
SELECT
  o.order_id,
  o.order_date,
  o.revenue,
  STRUCT(c.customer_id, c.segment, c.region) AS customer,
  STRUCT(p.product_id, p.category AS product_category, p.brand) AS product
FROM `raw.orders` o
JOIN `raw.customers` c USING (customer_id)
JOIN `raw.products` p USING (product_id);

bytes.topic: ELT vs ETL — why modern warehouses favor ELT, dbt as the transformation layer
bytes.title: BigQuery ELT — Why You Load Raw, Transform Later, and Let dbt Own the Logic
bytes.body:
Traditional ETL transforms data before loading — a relic of when compute was expensive and storage was scarce. BigQuery inverts this. With columnar storage, automatic clustering, and serverless query execution priced per byte scanned, it's cheaper and faster to load raw data into a dedicated dataset (e.g., `raw.ga4_events`) and transform it inside BigQuery using SQL.

dbt slots in as the transformation layer: it compiles `.sql` model files into `CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE` / `CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW` statements that run directly against BigQuery, manages a DAG of dependencies, and handles incremental materializations via `__dbt_tmp` staging tables. You get version-controlled, testable SQL instead of fragile pipeline scripts.

The key BigQuery-specific win: dbt incremental models use `MERGE` statements with `unique_key`, which maps to BigQuery's native DML. Combine this with partitioned tables on `event_date` and `require_partition_filter = true`, and you scan only the partitions dbt touches — keeping costs predictable as data volumes grow.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- models/marts/fct_ga4_sessions.sql
{{ config(
    materialized='incremental',
    unique_key='session_id',
    partition_by={'field': 'event_date', 'data_type': 'date'},
    cluster_by=['user_pseudo_id']
) }}

SELECT
    (SELECT value.int_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) WHERE key = 'ga_session_id') AS session_id,
    user_pseudo_id,
    DATE(TIMESTAMP_MICROS(event_timestamp)) AS event_date,
    COUNT(*) AS event_count
FROM {{ source('raw', 'ga4_events') }}
{% if is_incremental() %}
    WHERE DATE(TIMESTAMP_MICROS(event_timestamp)) >= DATE_SUB(CURRENT_DATE(), INTERVAL 3 DAY)
{% endif %}
GROUP BY 1, 2, 3

bytes.topic: Partitioning & Clustering — partition pruning, query performance, partition strategy
bytes.title: BigQuery Partitioning & Clustering — Cut Costs with Partition Pruning
bytes.body:
BigQuery charges by bytes scanned, so partition pruning is your first line of defense. When you filter on a partitioned column, BigQuery skips entire partition files — but only if your WHERE clause directly references that column without wrapping it in a function. `WHERE DATE(event_timestamp) = '2024-01-01'` disables pruning; `WHERE event_timestamp BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-02'` enables it.

For time-series tables, prefer `TIMESTAMP`-based partitioning over `DATE` ingestion time (`_PARTITIONTIME`) — it gives you explicit control and works with `partition_expiration_days` to auto-expire stale data. Layer clustering on top with up to 4 columns ordered by query filter frequency: `CLUSTER BY user_id, event_type` means filters on `user_id` alone still benefit, but `event_type` alone does not.

Use `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.PARTITIONS` to audit partition sizes and catch skew early. A single hot partition (e.g., a `NULL` catch-all) can silently destroy query performance. Set `require_partition_filter = TRUE` on large tables to enforce discipline across the team.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Partition pruning: good vs bad
-- BAD: function wrapping disables pruning
SELECT * FROM `project.dataset.events`
WHERE DATE(event_timestamp) = '2024-01-15';

-- GOOD: direct range filter enables pruning
SELECT * FROM `project.dataset.events`
WHERE event_timestamp >= '2024-01-15 00:00:00 UTC'
  AND event_timestamp <  '2024-01-16 00:00:00 UTC';

-- Inspect partition sizes for skew
SELECT partition_id, total_rows, total_logical_bytes
FROM `project.dataset.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.PARTITIONS`
ORDER BY total_logical_bytes DESC
LIMIT 10;

bytes.topic: Cost Management — query cost estimation, slot/credit consumption, materialized views
bytes.title: BigQuery Cost Management — Estimate Costs, Control Slots, and Cache with Materialized Views
bytes.body: Every BigQuery query on on-demand pricing costs $6.25/TB processed. Before running expensive queries, use INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS or dry-run execution to estimate bytes billed. Set `dry_run: true` in the job config — BigQuery validates the query and returns `totalBytesProcessed` without executing or charging. For capacity-based pricing, monitor slot utilization via INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS_BY_PROJECT: high slot contention means queries queue rather than fail, but latency spikes. Use `reservation_id` to isolate workloads. Materialized views are BigQuery's most underused cost lever — they pre-aggregate query results and are automatically used by the query optimizer (smart tuning) even if you don't reference them directly. A materialized view refreshes incrementally on base table DML, not full scans. Pair them with partition filters on the base table to cut refresh cost. For dashboards hitting the same aggregation repeatedly, a materialized view can drop per-query cost from hundreds of GBs to near zero.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Dry-run via bq CLI to estimate bytes before execution
-- bq query --dry_run 'SELECT user_id, SUM(spend) FROM `project.dataset.orders` WHERE DATE(created_at) = "2024-01-01" GROUP BY 1'

-- Check last 7 days slot usage and bytes billed per user
SELECT
  user_email,
  SUM(total_bytes_billed) / POW(1024, 4) AS tb_billed,
  SUM(total_slot_ms) / 1000 AS slot_seconds
FROM `region-us`.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS_BY_PROJECT
WHERE DATE(creation_time) >= DATE_SUB(CURRENT_DATE(), INTERVAL 7 DAY)
  AND job_type = 'QUERY'
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY tb_billed DESC
LIMIT 20;

bytes.topic: Data Quality — null handling, deduplication, constraint enforcement in warehouses
bytes.title: BigQuery Data Quality — Nulls, Deduplication, and Constraint Enforcement
bytes.body: BigQuery has no enforced PRIMARY KEY or UNIQUE constraints — they're declared as hints for the query optimizer, not guardrails. That means data quality is your responsibility at write time. For null handling, use COALESCE and IFNULL in your transformation layer, but lean on column-level NOT NULL mode in your schema definitions to reject bad rows at load time via the Storage Write API. For deduplication, the canonical pattern is a MERGE with a CTE that windows over your source using ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY id ORDER BY updated_at DESC), keeping only rank=1 rows before merging into the target table. Avoid SELECT DISTINCT on large tables — it forces a full shuffle. For constraint simulation, use information_schema.TABLE_CONSTRAINTS to document intent and combine with scheduled queries that assert COUNT(*) = COUNT(DISTINCT id), alerting via BigQuery scheduled query notifications or Dataplex data quality rules, which support row-level checks with SQL predicates and integrate with Cloud Logging for drift alerts.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Idempotent dedup MERGE for BigQuery
MERGE `project.dataset.orders` AS target
USING (
  SELECT * EXCEPT(rn)
  FROM (
    SELECT *,
      ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY order_id ORDER BY updated_at DESC) AS rn
    FROM `project.dataset.orders_staging`
  )
  WHERE rn = 1
) AS source
ON target.order_id = source.order_id
WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET target.updated_at = source.updated_at, target.status = source.status
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT ROW;

bytes.topic: Access Control — role-based access, column-level security, row-level policies
bytes.title: BigQuery Access Control — IAM, Column Policy Tags, and Row Access Policies
bytes.body:
BigQuery's access model stacks three independent layers. At the coarsest grain, IAM roles (`roles/bigquery.dataViewer`, `roles/bigquery.dataEditor`, `roles/bigquery.dataOwner`) bind to projects, datasets, or individual tables — table-level grants override dataset defaults without affecting sibling tables.

Column-level security runs through Data Catalog policy tags. You define a taxonomy, tag sensitive columns in the schema editor or via the API, then grant `roles/datacatalog.categoryFineGrainedReader` on the tag node. Unauthorized callers querying a tagged column receive NULL — no error, silent redaction.

Row-level security uses `CREATE ROW ACCESS POLICY`. Each policy declares a grantee list and a `FILTER USING` predicate evaluated per row. Multiple policies on the same table are OR'd — a session matching any policy sees the union of those rows. A session matching none sees zero rows, not an error. Use `SESSION_USER()` directly inside the predicate for per-identity filtering without a separate ACL table. All active policies are visible in `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.ROW_ACCESS_POLICIES`.

The three layers compose cleanly: IAM controls who can run queries at all, policy tags redact columns, row policies scope the visible row set.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Row access policy: US sales team sees only US orders
CREATE ROW ACCESS POLICY us_orders_filter
ON `myproject.sales.orders`
GRANT TO ("group:us-sales@company.com")
FILTER USING (region = 'US');

-- Per-user policy: each analyst sees only their own events
CREATE ROW ACCESS POLICY user_scoped_events
ON `myproject.analytics.events`
GRANT TO ("domain:company.com")
FILTER USING (owner_email = SESSION_USER());

-- Inspect active policies on a table
SELECT policy_name, grantee_list, filter_predicate
FROM `myproject.sales.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.ROW_ACCESS_POLICIES`
WHERE table_name = 'orders';

bytes.topic: Performance Optimization — result caching, materialized views, query optimization
bytes.title: BigQuery Performance Optimization — Caching, Materialized Views, and Slot Efficiency
bytes.body:
BigQuery's query result cache is automatic but often misunderstood. Results are cached for 24 hours per project, but only if the query is identical (byte-for-byte), references no non-deterministic functions like CURRENT_TIMESTAMP(), and the underlying tables haven't changed. Use this to your advantage in dashboards by parameterizing carefully.

For repeated aggregations over large partitioned tables, materialized views are the real win. BigQuery auto-refreshes them on DML changes and can serve dashboard queries directly from the materialized result — zero full-scan cost. Define them with a PARTITION BY clause that mirrors the base table to keep refresh incremental.

On the query side, always filter on partition columns first — BigQuery's partition pruning is aggressive but only fires on WHERE clauses referencing the partition column directly. Avoid wrapping partition columns in functions (DATE(ts) kills pruning; use ts BETWEEN instead). Use APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT() over COUNT(DISTINCT) for analytics at scale — it's 40–50x faster with <1% error. For slot-heavy joins, place the larger table on the left side of JOIN to guide the distributed shuffle.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Materialized view with incremental refresh via partitioning
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW analytics.daily_event_counts
PARTITION BY event_date
CLUSTER BY event_type
OPTIONS (enable_refresh = true, refresh_interval_minutes = 60)
AS
SELECT
  DATE(created_at) AS event_date,
  event_type,
  APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT(user_id) AS approx_unique_users,
  COUNT(*) AS total_events
FROM raw.events
WHERE created_at >= '2024-01-01'
GROUP BY 1, 2;

bytes.topic: Data Sharing — cross-account sharing, data marketplace, secure views
bytes.title: BigQuery Data Sharing — Authorized Views, Cross-Account IAM, and Analytics Hub
bytes.body:
BigQuery data sharing operates at three distinct tiers. Direct dataset sharing — grant `roles/bigquery.dataViewer` to a principal in another project and they can read every table in that dataset. For row or column filtering without exposing base tables, use authorized views: create the view in a separate dataset, register it as an authorized view on the source dataset via `bq update --add_view_access_policies`, and callers only see the view's output while the view itself queries the protected tables with elevated access — no base table IAM required for the consumer.

For cross-organization sharing at scale, Analytics Hub eliminates manual IAM sprawl. Publishers create a Data Exchange, attach Listings to source datasets, and subscribers link them — producing a read-only linked dataset inside the subscriber's own project. No data is copied; queries run against publisher storage and billing falls on the subscriber's project. Row access policies defined on the publisher's dataset propagate automatically to linked datasets.

Combine authorized views with `SESSION_USER()` for per-identity row filtering. For bulk policy propagation across many views, add an authorized dataset at the dataset level rather than registering each view individually.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- 1. Secure view with per-identity row filtering
CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW `proj.public_views.orders_summary` AS
SELECT order_id, customer_id, total_usd, region
FROM `proj.private_data.orders`
WHERE region IN (
  SELECT allowed_region
  FROM `proj.private_data.user_acl`
  WHERE user_email = SESSION_USER()
);

-- 2. Register the view as authorized on the source dataset (run in shell)
-- bq update \
--   --add_view_access_policies '[{"projectId":"proj","datasetId":"public_views","tableId":"orders_summary"}]' \
--   proj:private_data

bytes.topic: Incremental Loading — append vs upsert strategies, CDC, merge patterns
bytes.title: BigQuery Incremental Loading — Append, Upsert, and MERGE Patterns
bytes.body:
BigQuery's append-only model is the cheapest path: stream or batch-load new rows into a partitioned table, query with a `WHERE _PARTITIONTIME >= TIMESTAMP_SUB(...)` filter, and pay nothing for writes. It breaks the moment rows need to change.

For mutable sources, use the MERGE statement against a staging table. Load deltas into a temp/staging table (partitioned by `ingestion_time`), then MERGE into the target on your natural key. BigQuery MERGE scans full target partitions unless you add a `WHEN MATCHED AND target.updated_at < source.updated_at` guard and cluster on the merge key—without this, costs balloon on large tables.

For CDC (Debezium, Datastream), the canonical pattern is append raw change events (op: I/U/D, `_change_sequence`) into a raw layer, then materialize the latest state with a `ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY id ORDER BY _change_sequence DESC)` window query into a clean layer. This avoids MERGE entirely on the raw tier and keeps full audit history. Scheduled queries or dbt incremental models (`unique_key` + `merge` strategy) automate the final materialization step.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Upsert via MERGE with partition pruning
MERGE `project.dataset.orders` T
USING (
  SELECT * FROM `project.dataset.orders_staging`
  WHERE DATE(ingestion_time) = CURRENT_DATE()
) S
ON T.order_id = S.order_id
  AND T.created_date >= DATE_SUB(CURRENT_DATE(), INTERVAL 7 DAY) -- partition filter
WHEN MATCHED AND T.updated_at < S.updated_at THEN
  UPDATE SET T.status = S.status, T.updated_at = S.updated_at
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN
  INSERT (order_id, status, created_date, updated_at)
  VALUES (S.order_id, S.status, S.created_date, S.updated_at);

bytes.topic: Semi-structured Data — JSON/Parquet handling, schema evolution, nested types
bytes.title: BigQuery Semi-Structured Data — JSON Type, Nested STRUCTs, and Schema Evolution Without the Pain
bytes.body:
BigQuery's native JSON type (GA since 2023) stores payloads in a binary-encoded format — unlike STRING columns with JSON text, JSON_VALUE() and JSON_QUERY() operate on pre-parsed structure, avoiding repeated deserialization overhead at query time.

For nested data, BigQuery uses STRUCT and ARRAY types natively. When loading Parquet, group fields map to STRUCT and repeated fields map to ARRAY<T> automatically — no schema hinting required. Use bq load --autodetect or define the schema explicitly to preserve nesting depth across ingestion.

Schema evolution on existing tables uses ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS. You cannot surgically add subfields inside an existing STRUCT — instead, add a new top-level nullable column or use CREATE TABLE AS SELECT with EXCEPT() to reshape. Nullable top-level additions are metadata-only operations with no data rewrite.

When querying, JSON_VALUE() returns STRING scalars, JSON_QUERY() returns a JSON subobject, and JSON_VALUE_ARRAY() extracts string arrays cleanly. Combine UNNEST() with ARRAY<STRUCT<>> columns to explode repeated records into flat rows — BigQuery's optimizer pushes predicates into the UNNEST, so filter before unnesting to minimize scanned bytes.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Extract from native JSON type + unnest repeated STRUCT<>
SELECT
  e.event_id,
  JSON_VALUE(e.payload, '$.user_id')        AS user_id,
  JSON_VALUE(e.payload, '$.action')         AS action,
  attr.key,
  attr.value
FROM `project.dataset.events` e,
  UNNEST(e.attributes) AS attr  -- ARRAY<STRUCT<key STRING, value STRING>>
WHERE DATE(e.created_at) = CURRENT_DATE()
  AND JSON_VALUE(e.payload, '$.action') = 'purchase'
  AND attr.key = 'sku';

bytes.topic: Monitoring — query history, cost dashboards, slow query alerts
bytes.title: BigQuery Monitoring — Query History, Cost Dashboards, and Slow Query Alerts
bytes.body:
BigQuery's `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS` view is your primary observability surface. It exposes every job executed in your project — including bytes billed, slot milliseconds consumed, query text, and wall-clock duration. Querying it lets you build cost dashboards without any third-party tooling.

For cost tracking, aggregate `total_bytes_billed` grouped by `user_email` or `labels.key` to attribute spend. A single on-demand query billing 5 TB at $6.25/TB shows up immediately.

For slow query alerts, filter on `TIMESTAMP_DIFF(end_time, start_time, SECOND) > 60` and join with `total_slot_ms` to distinguish long-running queries from slot-starved ones — the fix is different for each.

To catch regressions automatically, schedule a BigQuery scheduled query that writes aggregated job stats to a summary table daily. Then connect Looker Studio or Grafana (via the BigQuery connector) for live dashboards. For real-time alerting, route `cloudaudit.googleapis.com/data_access` logs from Cloud Logging to Pub/Sub and trigger a Cloud Function when `jobStatistics.totalBilledBytes` exceeds your threshold.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Top 10 most expensive queries in the last 7 days
SELECT
  job_id,
  user_email,
  ROUND(total_bytes_billed / POW(1024, 4), 4) AS tb_billed,
  ROUND(total_bytes_billed / POW(1024, 4) * 6.25, 2) AS est_cost_usd,
  TIMESTAMP_DIFF(end_time, start_time, SECOND) AS duration_sec,
  total_slot_ms,
  SUBSTR(query, 1, 120) AS query_preview
FROM `region-us`.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS
WHERE creation_time >= TIMESTAMP_SUB(CURRENT_TIMESTAMP(), INTERVAL 7 DAY)
  AND job_type = 'QUERY'
  AND state = 'DONE'
  AND error_result IS NULL
ORDER BY total_bytes_billed DESC
LIMIT 10;

bytes.topic: Integration — connecting to dbt, Airflow, BI tools, reverse ETL
bytes.title: BigQuery Integration — Wiring dbt, Airflow, Looker, and Reverse ETL Into One Coherent Stack
bytes.body: BigQuery sits at the center of most modern data stacks, but the integration surface is where teams lose hours. With dbt, point your profiles.yml at BigQuery using the bigquery adapter — use oauth or service_account auth, set location explicitly (US vs EU matters for cross-region joins), and lean on dbt's built-in incremental strategy using _partitiontime for partitioned tables. For Airflow, skip the generic PythonOperator and use BigQueryInsertJobOperator from apache-airflow-providers-google — it submits jobs asynchronously and polls via the Jobs API, so you're not blocking a worker slot. BI tools like Looker use BigQuery's INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES and column-level caching — enable BI Engine reservation on your dataset to get sub-second cached results without rewriting queries. For reverse ETL (Census, Hightouch), grant the service account bigquery.dataViewer on source datasets and bigquery.jobUser at the project level — never project-wide editor. Use authorized views to expose transformed models without granting raw table access to third-party connectors.
bytes.code_snippet:
# dbt profiles.yml — BigQuery service account config
my_project:
  target: prod
  outputs:
    prod:
      type: bigquery
      method: service-account
      project: my-gcp-project
      dataset: analytics
      location: US
      keyfile: /secrets/sa-key.json
      threads: 8
      timeout_seconds: 300
      priority: interactive
      retries: 2

bytes.topic: When to Use What — Snowflake vs BigQuery vs Redshift vs Databricks decision framework
bytes.title: BigQuery vs Snowflake vs Redshift vs Databricks — A Decision Framework for BigQuery Teams
bytes.body:
BigQuery wins when your workload is ad-hoc, GCP-native, and query-heavy without predictable concurrency. Its serverless model means no cluster management—slots scale automatically, and you pay per TB scanned (on-demand) or commit to flat-rate reservations. Query `region-us.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS` to audit your 30-day spend and decide which pricing model fits.

Choose Snowflake when you need cross-cloud Secure Data Sharing or Time Travel beyond BigQuery's 7-day window. Snowflake's virtual warehouse sizing is more predictable for steady-state concurrent workloads with many small queries.

Choose Redshift when you're AWS-native and need tight integration with S3, Glue, and Spectrum for federated queries. RA3 nodes decouple storage from compute similarly to BigQuery, but within the AWS ecosystem.

Choose Databricks when ML is first-class—MLflow, Delta Live Tables, and Unity Catalog make it the strongest lakehouse for feature engineering pipelines. BigQuery ML (`CREATE MODEL`) handles in-warehouse regression and classification well, but Databricks wins for custom PyTorch/TensorFlow workflows.

BigQuery's irreplaceable differentiators: `BI Engine` for sub-second in-memory dashboard queries, `BigQuery Omni` for querying S3/Azure Blob without data movement, and native geospatial functions like `ST_GEOGPOINT` and `ST_DWITHIN`.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Audit 30-day spend to decide on-demand vs flat-rate reservations
SELECT
  user_email,
  COUNT(*)                                                        AS job_count,
  ROUND(SUM(total_bytes_billed) / POW(1024, 4), 4)               AS total_tb_billed,
  ROUND(SUM(total_bytes_billed) / POW(1024, 4) * 6.25, 2)        AS est_cost_usd
FROM `region-us`.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS
WHERE
  creation_time BETWEEN TIMESTAMP_SUB(CURRENT_TIMESTAMP(), INTERVAL 30 DAY)
    AND CURRENT_TIMESTAMP()
  AND job_type = 'QUERY'
  AND state    = 'DONE'
GROUP BY user_email
ORDER BY total_tb_billed DESC
LIMIT 10;
